entry.title: Supply Protection Extension Rule in SAP S/4 HANA
entry.content:
In this blog we will see how supply protection extension rules can be configured to modify the consumption and restriction behavior of protection groups and their corresponding time buckets.

Pre-Requisites:

  • Product version is S/4 HANA Private Cloud Edition 2023 FPS03 / or above and Fashion business function is activated
  • Self-Restriction type is set as ‘Restriction Outside Planned Protection’

Solution Context:

Supply Protection Extension Rule aims in addressing below business requirements:

  1. Consider protected quantities of backward and forward time buckets while calculating remaining protection for the matching time buckets
  2. Consume protection from backward and forward time buckets
  3. Ignore restrictions applied due to horizontal protection up to remaining protection and consider them only for the required quantity exceeding remaining protection.

Below Example illustrates a scenario where Supply Protection Extension Rules are crucial in defining the consumption from multiple time buckets.

 

 

Feb

Mar

Apr

May

 

Supply

 

 

On Hand Stock

250

 

 

 

 

Inbound Delivery

 

225

 

 

 

Purchase Order

 

 

225

 

 

Purchase Requisition

 

 

 

225

 

 

 

Supply Protection

 

Priority

Protection Group

 

 

 

 

1

ECOM

50

50

50

50

1

RETAIL

75

75

75

75

2

Key Wholesale

100

100

100

100

 

General Availability

25

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Its important to set the right “Self-Restriction” Type in above case to ensure a specific channel doesn’t overconsume the supply. For e.g. if we set “Self-Restriction” as “No Self-Restriction” then ECOM and RETAIL channels can consume all of key wholesale protection which is not always intended.

We will review this example with “Self-Restriction” set as “Outside Planned Protection” which aims to ensure even low priority protection groups get their share of supply.

 Step 1: Create Supply Protection Extension Rule

  • Go to Fiori App “Configure Supply Protection Extension Rules”
  • Click on “Create” and enter below configuration required in the rule

  • Lets us understand the specifics of each field
    • General Information:
      • Rule Name – Provide a name for the extension rule to be used in “Manage Supply Protection” Fiori App.
    • Restriction Horizon:
      • Format – Here you can specify if you can borrow units from forward and backward time buckets in “Days” or “Time buckets”
      • Backward Period – How many backward time buckets/days you can borrow/consume units from the matching time bucket
      • Forward Period – How many forward time buckets/days you can borrow/consume units from the matching time bucket
    • Consumption:
      • Consumption Reference Type: Here you can specify if you want to consume based on “standard” (i.e. all units consumed only from matching time bucket) or “Restriction Horizon” (apply backward/forward horizons for consumption)
      • Consumption Direction: currently 2 types of consumption directions are support –
        • Backward then Forward – System consumes in sequence: Matching time bucket -> Matching time bucket – 1 -> Matching time bucket – 2 (upto X number of backward time buckets) -> Matching time bucket + 1 -> Matching time bucket + 2 (upto X number of forward time buckets). Any left-over unconsumed quantities after above sequence will be consumed in matching time bucket. For. E.g in above example, if ECOM Sales Order comes in March for 160 units, then system consumes in sequence March (50) -> Feb (50) -> April (50) -> March (10). Final consumptions will be Feb (50), March (60), April (50).
        • Forward then Backward – System consumes in sequence: Matching time bucket -> Matching time bucket + 1 -> Matching time bucket + 2 (upto X number of forward time buckets) -> Matching time bucket - 1 -> Matching time bucket - 2 (upto X number of backward time buckets). Any leftover unconsumed quantities after above sequence will be consumed in matching time bucket. For. E.g in above example, if ECOM Sales Order comes in March for 160 units, then system consumes in sequence March (50) -> April (50) -> Feb (50) -> March (10). Final consumptions will be Feb (50), March (60), April (50).
      • Priority Restriction:
        • Special Handling of Equal Priority – This indicator specifies if restrictions of “equal priority” should be considered only in the “out plan” quantity or both “in plan and out plan” quantities. i.e. If an ECOM order comes for 200 units, then “in plan” quantity is “150” and out plan is “50”:
          • If flag is not set – restriction of “Retail” protection for 300 units will be applicable for both “in plan” and “out plan” quantities
          • If flag is set – restriction of “Retail” protection for 300 units will be applicable only for “out plan” quantities

Step 2: Attach Supply Protection Extension Rule to Supply Protection Object

  • Go to Fiori App “Manage Supply Protection” and define below parameters as shown below: (Note – Attach the extension rule)
  • Note - Set Self Restriction Type as "Restriction Outside Planned Protection"

 

 

  • Activate the supply protection object

Case 1: Let’s take an example where ECOM Sales Order comes in for 200 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since Restriction Horizon is set as “1” for both forward and backward, system will now consider remaining protection for March as “150” (including Feb and April). So, the 200 ECOM order request is checked in 2 steps as shown below:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

In Plan Quantity

150

No restrictions applied

150

Out Plan Quantity

50

-          Equal Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

-          Low Priority – 400 units from Key Wholesale

-          Self-Restriction – 50 from ECOM April

25 from General Availability

Final Confirmations

 

 

175 units

Case 2: Let’s take an example where RETAIL STO comes in for 200 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since Restriction Horizon is set as “1” for both forward and backward, system will now consider remaining protection for March as “225” (including Feb and April). So, the 200 RETAIL order request is checked in single step:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

In Plan Quantity

200

No restrictions applied

200

Final Confirmations

 

 

200 units

Case 3: Let’s take an example where Key Wholesale Sales Order comes in for 500 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since Restriction Horizon is set as “1” for both forward and backward, system will now consider remaining protection for March as “300” (including Feb and April). So, the 500 Key Wholesale order request checked in two steps as shown below:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

In Plan Quantity

300

-          High Priority – 200 units from ECOM

-          High Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

300

Out Plan Quantity

200

-          High Priority – 200 units from ECOM

-          High Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

-          Self-Restriction – 100 from Key Wholesale April

25 from General Availability

Final Confirmations

 

 

325 units

Case 4: Let’s take an example where regular Wholesale Sales Order comes in for 500 comes for March.

With Self Restriction set as “Restriction Outside Planned Protection” and extension rule is set as shown above, below are the expected results and explanation.

Since there is no protection for regular wholesale order, all 500 units will be checked in single step:

 

 

Restrictions

Confirmations

Request Quantity

500

-          High Priority – 200 units from ECOM

-          High Priority – 300 units from RETAIL

-          High Priority – 400 units from Key Wholesale

25 from General Availability

Final Confirmations

 

 

25 units

Hope this blog helps to provide a clear understanding of “Supply Protection Extension Rules” to define appropriate settings to prioritize different channels as per business requirements.

SAP Help Documentation:

  1. Supply Protection
  2. Supply Protection Extension Rule

SAP Community Blogs:

  1. Supply Protection In a Nutshell
  2. Increasing Reliability with Supply Protection
  3. Restriction by Own Protection in Supply Protection
  4. Insights on Supply Protection using Temporary Assignments

entry.title: Stock/Requirements List with Segmentation Mapping (MD04P) and Custom Fixed Pegging
entry.content:
[Blog Type: Hands-on Expertise]

 

Summary

This blog post describes how the stock/requirements pegging list (transaction MD04P) works and which additional pegging information is displayed. A custom fixed pegging tool is also presented for a customer where supply assignment did not fully cover its requirements, and the integration of the fixed elements on the MD04P is also explained.

The target audience for this blog will be consulting people, project team members, key users, and subject matter experts who are interested in cross features like segmentation in SAP S/4HANA.

 

Introduction

Segmentation is a mean to divide the materials stock into named segments that can be distinguished in inventory management and in many logistic processes, e.g. in sales orders, in production orders, in purchase orders, in inspection lots, etc… With segmentation, you can classify materials or products based on their physical and logical characteristics, without increasing the total number of materials, BOMs and routings in the system.

Image 1. Examples of segmentation.

The key scenario where segmentation plays a major role is in make-to-stock (MTS) scenarios where customers require processing stocks/receipts linked to sales order line items but without using individual customer stocks, as required per make-to-order (MTO) scenarios. There are multiple benefits of using segmentation over an MTO scenario, like flexibility to manage processes and ease of transaction, to name a few. You can refer to the SAP Press E-Bite "Introducing Supply and Demand Segmentation in SAP S/4HANA" for more information.

Segmentation was originally developed for the fashion industry, and in SAP S/4HANA 2020 was introduced as a cross feature under supply and demand segmentation in the on-premise / private cloud edition. Segmentation is not available for the public cloud edition. Segmentation is not part of the standard SAP S/4HANA enterprise license: a dedicated license for segmentation is required if you are not using the SAP S/4HANA for fashion and vertical business license.

Segmentation reduces significantly the number of master data objects by associating attributes rather than creating additional master data. Segmentation works on characteristics-based concepts like variant configuration, but it is different from any other characteristics-based solution. Segmentation setup is outlined on a segmentation structure and segmentation strategy that is applied to the material master.

Image 2. Segmentation configuration.

Image 3. Segmentation data on material master.

Segmentation structure is defined using characteristics that manifest themselves as requirement fields and stock fields in the structure. The characteristics have relevance on both the stock side and the requirement side.

Image 4. Segmentation structure definition on segmentation setup transaction.

The segmentation strategy is based on the mapping between a stock segment associated with a material in stock and a requirement segment associated with a demand object. Segmentation strategies can be assigned to materials to determine the valid stock segments and requirement segments for the materials and to define which requirements should be covered by which stock segments. Mapping of requirement segment and stock segment can be 1:N or N:M.

Image 5. Segmentation strategy definition on segmentation setup transaction.

You must specify the segmentation values that are comprehensible and easy to enter into the system. Each segmentation value must be a combination of characteristic values that you have previously defined in classification characteristic maintenance.

Image 6. Valid segmentation values definition on segmentation setup transaction.

As segmentation is part of the material master, it is applicable to all transactions or apps across all modules integrated with materials management. The value of the segmentation field can be defined by the user to indicate either a physical segmentation or a logical segmentation, and a material can have n numbers of segmentation values. However, in a transaction for a material code, you can specify only one of the allowed segmentation values.

There are two types of segmentation, requirement segments and stock segments:

  • A requirement segment indicates SAP documents relevant to demand, such as sales orders, PIRs, and STOs. The requirement segment subdivides requirements based on specific criteria, such as the quality or the customer segments.
  • A stock segment indicates SAP documents that are relevant to supply, such as existing or planned stocks. Stock segments are assigned to the physical stock and to the procurement proposals (for example, planned orders, production orders, purchase requisitions, or purchase orders).

Image 7. Example of demand and supply elements carrying segments.

The values of the requirement and stock segments should be the same to establish the connection between them, when no mapping rule is maintained. You also can use multilevel requirement segments and stock segment connections; the values might not be the same, but SAP has the intelligence to peg the stock segments to requirement segments based on mapping rules configured on segmentation strategy, as explained before.

Segments are internally built using batches and their characteristics. Batch management is mandatory if the material is relevant for segmentation and the segmentation strategy scope is “1” (requirement and stock segments maintained). Therefore, once the segmentation is assigned to the material master, the batch functionality would be turned on automatically in case not yet activated.

Image 8. Example of stock segment assigned to material batch on batch master data.

You can find a record of all segment mapping with the most up-to-date stock and requirements situation in the cross-industry report called Stock/Requirements Pegging List app (transaction MD04P).

This blog explains how the stock/requirements pegging list works and which additional pegging information is displayed. Furthermore, a custom fixed pre-assignment tool is presented for a customer where supply assignment (ARun) did not fully cover its requirements, and the integration between MD04P and the fixed pre-assigned information is also be explained.

 

Stock/Requirements Pegging List

In the stock/requirements pegging list, the system displays the most up-to-date stock and requirements situation, similar to transaction MD04 but with pegging information derived from segmentation. You can use it to view the stock/requirements situation for the individual segments of a material with segmentation, thus providing end-to-end supply and demand visibility for a material plant.

The name pegging comes from PP/DS and states for a procedure that establishes the relationship between receipt elements and requirements elements of a product within a location. With pegging, the corresponding receipt elements are assigned to the requirements, as happens in segmentation. Therefore, MD04P is a pegging transaction where the requirements are mapped to stocks based on the ascending order of delivery date (requirement) and plan date (receipts).

This app has evolved from the SAP S/4HANA for fashion and business vertical solution; it has now become a cross-industry stock/requirement list app as of the 2022 release version of SAP S/4HANA. Unlike the classic stock/requirement list transaction (MD04), where there was no demand element to supply element mapping by requirement segment and stock segment mapping, the new app provides these details.

It’s worth mentioning that segmentation-based planning can only be done through MRP Live (based on SAP HANA); classic MRP does not support segmented material planning. MRP Live determines which requirement segment can be fulfilled by which stock segment. Each time you access the list, the system selects the various MRP elements and displays the most up-to-date situation. Any changes that have been made after the last planning date are also displayed and, therefore, the list is dynamic.

Image 9. Overview of MRP Live process with segmentation.

The selection screen contains the input material, MRP area, and plant in the main screen. The additional filters are available for MRP Statuses (explained later), Stock Element, Stock Number, Requirement Element and Requirement Number. It is also possible to set the validity in the future from today on date field “Valid to”, feature which was not available on MD04.

Image 10. Example of selection screen with individual access for MD04P.

The MD04P report is available for individual access and collective access. When using the collective access, filtering by several material-specific fields is possible. As result of the collective access, all selected materials are displayed on the “Overview Tree”.

Image 11. Example of selection screen with collective access for MD04P.

You can execute the stock/requirements pegging list for all materials with the display tree enabled. By double-clicking a material code, you can see the requirement segment and stock segment mapping sorted by connected demand and supply elements. Therefore, for every material/plant combination, you will see a detailed sequence of requirements and receipts over the time which respects the segmentation strategies. In detail, you will see the pegging quantities per receipt or requirement item.

Image 12. Example of ALV display with overview tree of material/plant combinations for MD04P.

The MD04P report displays a user-friendly ALV screen, so it can be transferred to Excel in tab file format. The ALV display list maps the appropriate stock elements to the requirement elements for the individual stock segments and displays them in separate tabs according to the MRP statuses. The system considers the segmentation strategy in the mappings.  Specifically, MD04P considers the rules maintained in the “ARun/MRP: Requirement/Stock Allocation” rule for mapping the requirement to stock.

The main screen has several tabs and an optional details screen like the one in MD04. On the details screen, an overview of the material master data is also available for the material/plant combination, by using the button to expand and collapse the material header details data.

Image 13. Example of ALV display with expanded header area for MD04P.

The main screen displays the information about how stock/supply elements are planned to be used (result of MRP run). This screen is separated into different tabs according to the MRP statuses, and the main information available is:

  • The free available stock (non-consumed)
  • The blocked requirements (non-assigned)
  • The consumed stock (assigned requirements and consumed stock)

The free available stock is the valid available stock that has not yet been assigned to any requirement. The blocked requirements are any demand that has not yet been covered by any existing stock. The consumed stock indicates for which demands the stock is planned to be used and what requirement segment they have.

Image 14. Example of ALV display with stock/supply elements grouped by MRP status for MD04P.

The main information displayed on the different tabs is the following:

  • Tab “A – Free Available Stock” shows the valid available stock (batch stock, purchase requisition, planned order, production, and so on) that has not yet been assigned to requirement.
  • Tab “B – MRP Blocked Requirements” shows any demand/requirement that has not yet been covered by any existing stock.
  • Tab “T – Temporarily Assigned Stock/Requirements” shows the list of the requirement elements against stock elements (catering elements) in one line. After MRP run, the stock element is updated for every requirement element. The other planning details like dates and material are also available on the same line.
  • Tab “X – Excluded requirements” shows requirements that are not considered during MRP in a make-to-stock scenario, for information purposes only.
  • Tab “∑ – Stock/Requirement Overview” gives more details about the requirement element. Summary of information available from all the tab strips.
  • Tab “I – Additional Information” gives the summarized details of the stock elements and required elements. In this view, users can see the overall picture for the planning situation along with excluded stock for planning.

You can navigate to the corresponding documents in display or change mode based on the MRP element type. For example, if the MRP element is “PrdOrd”, you can double-click the requirement number and navigate to the corresponding Production Order (display transaction CO03 and change transaction CO02).

Other functions are also accessible from the top menu of MD04P report, like order conversion or release purchase requisition. Navigation to the Display Total Requirements and Stock Overview screens is also possible.

Image 15. Example of functions accessible from the top menu for MD04P.

When executing the MRP Live (transaction MD01N) on foreground mode, you can also navigate to the Stock/Requirements Pegging List (MD04P) besides the Stock/Requirements List (MD04).

Image 16. Navigate to MD04P as result of MRP Live execution.

MD04P report contains some specific features (for instance, seasons) which are only available for the industry solution SAP S/4HANA for Fashion and Vertical Business. They are only displayed with specific fashion transaction FSH_MD04.

 

Behavior of Stock/Requirement List (MD04) with Segmentation

When the Stock/Requirements List (MD04) is executed for a segmented material, the requirement segment is in the stock/requirement list table’s last column, and the stock segment is in the header level right after the plant code. This means that you can check all the stock elements and their pegged requirements for a single stock segment, which may be linked to the same or a different requirement segment.

The complete overview can be loaded for a material and plant for all stock segments or a specific stock segment. If a specific stock segment is entered as a filter on selection screen, only supply elements with that stock segment are listed.

Image 17. Example of MD04 display for segmented material.

 

Custom Fixed Pre-Assignment Tool

Functionality of Supply Assignment (ARun) can be activated by a business function and provides an optimized assignment of existing stock to open requirements by creating a fixed pre-assignment that remains till outbound delivery is created. In a supply shortage situation, supply pre-assignment ensures an optimal assignment of supply to the open requirements and distributes available supply to the respective demand (sales orders, stock transport orders, etc…)

The stock/requirements pegging list (MD04P) is enhanced with Supply Assignment (ARun) status details on separate tabs, according to the different ARun available statuses. Here a list with all the pre-assigned stock batches to the different requirements is displayed.

Customer was not only interested in pre-reservations against stock batches, but also in pre-reservations against planned orders and pre-reservations against production orders. Moreover, greater flexibility for manual assignations was also requested. Therefore, the true challenge was

  1. To develop a new app for fast manual linking of stock and receipt elements with requirement elements, whilst strictly respecting the segmentation mapping rules.
  2. To enable the MRP Live for not considering the already pre-assigned requirements and stock/receipt elements for the netting calculation (the so-called fixed elements or fixed pegging).
  3. To integrate the already fixed pre-assigned requirements-stocks into the stock/requirements pegging list.

Therefore, a new app called “Fixed Pegging Tool” was created for allowing a fast manual linking of stock/receipt elements with requirement elements, whilst strictly respecting the segmentation mapping rules.

Image 18. Selection screen of Fixed Pegging Tool.

Image 19. Main screen of Fixed Pegging Tool.

The main screen of the new app shows some header information and two main ALV displays separated by vertical splitter. The left ALV displays the requirement elements grouped by temporarily assigned and already pre-reserved requirements. The right ALV displays the stock/receipt elements.

By clicking on a single requirement segment, the cell becomes green color and valid stock segments based on the segmentation mapping rules are also highlighted on green color, thus helping the user to identify the possible valid lines to be linked to particular requirement.

Image 20. Valid stock segments for single requirement segment on Fixed Pegging Tool.

On the other hand, by clicking on a single stock segment, the cell becomes green color and valid requirement segments based on the segmentation mapping rules are also highlighted on green color, thus helping the user to identify the possible valid lines to be linked to a specific batch or planned order or production order.

Image 21. Valid requirement segments for single stock segment on Fixed Pegging Tool.

Once the user has decided the records to be linked, buttons “Link Orders” and “Unlink Orders” can be used to link left with right records or unlink them.

Image 22. Buttons for linking and unlinking orders on Fixed Pegging Tool.

When one requirement is linked, the old MRP status “Temporarily Assigned Requirements” becomes into the new status “Reserved Requirement” on MD04P (fixed element).

Image 23. Example of linked requirement with production order on Fixed Pegging Tool.

We could establish again a parallelism between the pegging information derived from segmentation and the fixed pegging derived from custom pre-assignment tool with the dynamic and fixed pegging procedures used for establishing pegging relationships in PP/DS. Dynamic pegging would correspond to the pegging information derived from segmentation while fixed pegging would correspond to fixed pre-assigned information coming from the custom tool.

Technically, the reservation takes place on RESB table with an append structure containing the planned order or production order number, as batch number is already available by standard.

Image 24. Example of linked requirement with production order on RESB table.

The already fixed pre-assigned requirements are displayed in the stock/requirements pegging list (MD04P) on existing tab “R – Supply Assignment: On Reservation”. The reserved stock element is also displayed and “Element is fixed” column is marked. This is achieved by implementing BAdI BADI_FSH_STOCK_REQ_LIST_AMDP.

Image 25. Example of linked requirement with production order on MD04P.

Finally, BAdIs BADI_FSH_STOCK_REQ_LIST_APPL and BADI_FSH_STOCK_REQ_LIST_UI are also available. With the first one, you can add new fields or change any existing fields that are relevant for the stock element or the requirement element in the stock/requirements pegging list. With the second one, you can define the display layout such as field catalog and sorting criteria for each of the MRP statuses (tabs) of the stock/requirements pegging list.

 

Helpful Links

Monitor Stock/Requirements List (SAP Fiori Apps Library)

Stock/Requirements Pegging (SAP Help Portal)

Incident component for clarifications:

  • Transaction MD04P: LO-RFM-PP
  • Supply Assignment (ARun): LO-RFM-ARN

For further details on SAP S/4HANA segmentation, you can also refer to the SAP Press E-Bite "Introducing Supply and Demand Segmentation in SAP S/4HANA".

 

[Disclaimer: Written by Juan Ventura (LinkedIn). This blog post is written by the author based on his personal experience while working on customers with SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud. Opinions and views expressed herein are solely my own. Before implementing any solution, you are advised to always do the analysis and adopt a solution for specific business case only if it suits customer requirements and testing is successful.]

entry.title: Company Code Carve out or Separation in SAP ERP system (SAP ECC & S4HANA On-Prem,Cloud environments)
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog outlines the typical business triggers that drive the need for Company Code Carve out in a productive ERP environment (ECC or S4HANA systems). It also highlights the possible realization methodologies to support such business transformations effectively.

Company Code Carve Out

A Company Code Carve-Out refers to the selective separation of data from a productive SAP instance into a separate target system. This data separation is driven by the organizational unit “Company Code” (technical domain: BUKRS) within the SAP system.

As part of the process, both higher-level organizational elements (e.g., Controlling Area, Operating Concern etc.) and lower-level hierarchical elements (e.g., Plant, Sales Organization, etc.) within the SAP enterprise structure are identified and analyzed to enable the separation of the entire legal entity’s business application data.

The dataset in scope typically includes all relevant data stored in SAP database tables and fields across all layers Viz. Customizing, Master, and Transactional data. This also encompasses customer-specific developments stored in Z or Y namespace tables.

Goal of Company Code Carve out is to give the divested business entity into an independent environment while minimizing the impact on the source system that contains the remaining data related to the relevant company codes.

Typical business use cases that require Company Code Carve out 

  1. Divesture of a Business Unit: It happens when the organization or group decides to sell off/dispose or close a part of the organization or subsidiary in order to raise cash/address regulatory or performance issues. With this activity, divested group can either become a stand-alone entity or independent company or strategic asset for a buyer.

  2. Country or Region-specific Separations/Decentralization of Central System: When an organization decides to spin-off or separates the country or region from their existing ECC or S4HANA landscape. These situations are primarily driven by the differences in the corporate laws, securities, regulations, tax rules and controls related to that country jurisdiction. Sometimes, this may also be driven due to the sanctions amended on specific countries leading to damage to the business in whole.

  3. Joint Venture: When organizations or partners decide to enter into JV with specific scope & duration to access country specific local markets, shared investments, risk diversifications and later decides to divest or spin off into a separate legal entity.

  4. Sale of Subsidiaries: An event occurs when parent organization plans to sale/transfer controls of a legal entity to another organization based on the board approvals, share holders’ resolution etc., for additional liquidity or strategic alignment on existing business.

  5. Strategic Refocus and Legal Restructuring: This kind of activity defines the business and reshapes the legal entity structure to support the strategy that organization thinks to refocusing their business. It helps in reallocation of capital to high advantage business or withdraw from misaligned businesses or non-core portfolio.

  6. Merger & Acquisitions: Mergers & Acquisitions are the transactions where organizations either combine or takes control over other to achieve strategic objectives/growth perspective. It provides opportunities to accelerate growth, portfolio diversification or consolidation.

All the aforementioned use cases require the complete historical transactional data of the respective legal entity to ensure uninterrupted business operations and to meet statutory and compliance reporting obligations.

Seamless operation and continuity of connected business applications are critical success factors for any divested or separated entity. Ensuring the continuity of open processes is essential for maintaining reporting dependencies and enabling informed strategic decision-making.

Furthermore, statutory reporting and audit trails of business application data represent critical data assets for any organization. They are fundamental to meeting country-specific data residency requirements and safeguarding the organization against potential legal and compliance risks arising from non-adherence to regulatory obligations.

To perform the separation of business entities/units (Company Code) from an existing SAP ERP landscape (ECC or S4HANA), SAP-DMLT service supports with SAP Company Code Carve Out solution that is tailored to address the aforementioned use cases with a cleaner strategy and data set that can let the organizations function independently and enables stable operations, financial reporting, auditing with the legal obligations.

Typically, objects in scope would be Company Codes and its relevant upper and lower hierarchical organizational elements (such as Controlling Area, Plant, Sales Org, associated master data – Customers, Vendors & Materials etc.)

Technical approach of Company Code Carve out

1. Company Code Clone & Delete

In this approach, the entire source system should be copied technically (clone) including repository objects, customizations, users and the business application data. Later, based on the user provided/confirmed company codes will be retained and all other company codes along with relevant org. units will be deleted from the cloned environment using SAP DMLT toolset.

This will technically delete the out-of-scope Company Codes along with its associated & shared data from target/cloned system, enables faster, cleaner and minimum disruption to business.

Business Outcomes (Value)

  1. Faster, Cleaner approach for Company Code Carve-out
  2. Reduced risk due to functional completeness
  3. Standard SAP tool to perform the carve out activity
  4. Limited testing required compared to general green field approach of only open balances
  5. Business Process Continuity & Faster realization
  6. Data history for statutory reporting needs

Transformation Scenario Limitations

  1. Suitable for smaller database
  2. Any additional data transformation expectation can't be embedded together

2. Company Code Transfer/Migration

As the name suggests, in this approach we will transfer / migrate only the relevant Company Codes with associated org. units along with the respective data sets only to the target empty system. It avoids the need for full copy of the source system compared to earlier explained 'Clone & Delete' approach.

P.S: Shell creation is the process of building a new & empty SAP system with complete repository, cross client objects and client specific customizing of source system without master & transaction data

Business Outcomes (Value)

  1. Minimal data footprint
  2. Lower infrastructure costs/repurpose any existing landscape
  3. Better control on the data set & objects (selection of relevant application) to be moved to target system/environment
  4. Possible to accommodate qualified transformations in any data harmonization (such as Rename of org objects, master data)
  5. Best suited for scenarios with a limited number of company codes being divested or for any country-specific carve outs - where the data set to be separated represents only a small or minimal portion of the overall database volume

Transformation Scenario Limitations

  1. Would require a longer project duration compared to clone-and-delete approach, as each application must be handled individually and might require additional, specific transformation capabilities embedded in the transfer logic.
  2. Relatively higher effort investment on validations and reconciliations

Realization Methodology of Company Code Carve-Out

Once a company code is productive or live with transactional data, any changes become restrictive. Moving configuration changes without transforming the already posted documents can lead to data inconsistencies in the live system, potentially causing critical disruptions in open business processes.

To separate any Company Code(s) from the live system, both the configuration, master and it’s associated transactional data must be handled consistently. For deletion of existing posted documents or transforming open documents, the SAP DMLT Company Code Carve Out Service is required. For more information, refer to the Company Code Carve Out Solution Brief.

Such data transformations in a live production system demand a highly precise and controlled changeover, ensuring consistency at the database level and seamless continuity of SAP software operations. Therefore, every relevant business scenario should be carefully reviewed in the system, considering current configurations and customizations across all connected application modules.

SAP DMLT offers a technical feasibility study to outline system data dependencies and prerequisite conditions. This is followed by a detailed customer workshop with the obtained statistics from the system. The realization of the transformation requirement is delivered under a separate professional consulting contract and executed as a project.

The overall delivery process is broadly divided into two parts:

1. Pre-Qualification of Requirement

It is essential to pre-qualify the business requirement to ensure the right-fit transformation solution is offered. This starts with gathering preliminary details about the business requirement and application usage within the system through a technical questionnaire, available at Mergers and Acquisitions, Divestments, Restructuring.

Based on the received responses, SAP can propose a feasibility study to assess the system, evaluate overall complexity, and deliver a detailed proposal. This assessment typically takes around two weeks, including the deployment of analysis tools in the system.

An SAP Solution Architect/Expert will perform the system assessment and present the key insights during a customer workshop session. This is followed by the provision of commercial details through the designated local SAP Market Unit account representatives.

2. Project Delivery

Depending on the complexity of the engagement, the required number of mock execution cycles will be determined during the scoping phase. In general, data transformation projects of this nature involve a minimum of 2-3 mock cycles before the production data cutover. The purpose of a mock execution is to simulate the complete production data carve-out/deletion, validate the results, and estimate the overall downtime required for the production system.

Each mock execution cycle includes several phases, which can be broadly classified as follows:

2.1 Technical Preparation Phase of Add-On (Uptime):

These types of transformation capabilities are deployed into the customer system as a separate add-on, delivered as a transportable content. The mock execution system should be a recent copy of the production system, with identical or closely matching hardware configuration that of the production environment. Once the tool content is deployed, the technical preparation phase begins, during which SAP DMLT experts prepare the transformation tool. This phase typically takes 2–3 weeks and includes uptime activities and the generation of technical programs for the relevant table-fields.

2.2 Data Carve-Out Phase (Downtime):

After the uptime preparatory activities are completed, the agreed business downtime begins. During this period, Company Code Carve-Out is performed directly at the database table-field level. All relevant Company Codes are separated along with the relevant Org Units in the respective application areas (e.g., FI-GL, FI-AA, ML, CO etc).

2.3 Post-Processing Testing Phase (Uptime):

Once the data carve-out phase is completed, the transformed system is handed over to the customer’s IT/application team for a sanity check during downtime. Upon successful completion of the sanity check, the transformed system is reopened for normal business operations.

Engagement with relevant SAP team or SAP Experts

If your business requirement involves any transformation related to Company Code aspects in a live, productive SAP ECC or S/4HANA system, you can engage with the SAP Data Management and Landscape Transformation (DMLT) consulting team through following ways;

  1. Create a Support Incident
    Submit an incident via the SAP ONE Support Launchpad under the component: CA-LT-SRV (Landscape Transformation Services), Or
  2. Contact via Email
    Reach out directly to the DMLT Global Consulting Experts Email: sap_dmlt_gce@sap.com

The SAP DMLT team will guide you through the pre-assessment process, feasibility evaluation, and project-based service engagement tailored to your specific system and transformation needs.

Reference SAP OSS Notes:

481938 - System Landscape Optimization Services

1462004 - SAP LT: Transformation Solution 'Company Carve-out'

Disclaimer: This blog is intended only for knowledge sharing about Company Code Carve-out/Delete service on SAP ERP (ECC or S/4 HANA) system which might help customer in their landscape transformation requirements. It should not be considered as guideline for performing such transformation services on their own in any of the SAP landscape. Any data transformation efforts should be thoroughly assessed in consultation with qualified subject matter experts, supported by a comprehensive system study, and aligned with the appropriate approvals from your auditors and relevant stakeholders.

entry.title: How SAP ESM + Document AI + S/4HANA Work Together to Automate Invoice Processing End‑to‑End
entry.content:
One of the most powerful capabilities in SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) is its ability to automate invoice processing the moment a customer or vendor sends an email. With SAP Document Information Extraction (Document AI) integrated into ESM, the journey from a raw invoice PDF to a validated posting in SAP S/4HANA Private or Public Edition becomes almost fully automated.
Click on Image to view High Quality
Click on Image to view High Quality

Step 1: The User Sends an Email with an Invoice

A customer or vendor sends an email to the SAP ESM dedicated email address configured for automatic case creation.

The invoice may be:

  • Attached as a PDF, JPEG, PNG, or
  • Embedded in the email body itself

SAP ESM picks up both.

Step 2: SAP ESM Automatically Creates a Case

Once the email reaches the ESM mailbox:

  • A new case is created automatically
  • The email body and all attachments are stored within the case
  • The attachments (PDFs, images) trigger SAP Document AI processing immediately

This eliminates manual case creation entirely.

Step 3: SAP Document AI Processes the Invoice

SAP Document AI retrieves the attachment from the case and begins extraction using OCR and machine learning.

It identifies:

  • Invoice number
  • Customer or vendor name
  • Address
  • Line items
  • Totals & taxes
  • Payment terms
  • Contract or PO references

Document AI also calculates a confidence score for each extracted field.
These scores help determine whether the data is reliable enough to auto‑post or requires user intervention.

Step 4: Validation Using Business Rules & Customer Framework

Once extraction is complete, SAP ESM applies:

  • Business rules
  • Customer validation frameworks
  • Mapping logic
  • Master data checks

These validations are essential before communicating with S/4HANA.

Examples:

  • Is the customer known in S/4?
  • Is the invoice number valid and not duplicated?
  • Does the PO exist?
  • Do line item totals match?

Only when the validations pass does ESM proceed to the next step.

Step 5: Invoice Number & Customer Auto‑Identification

If Document AI finds the invoice number, SAP ESM updates the line items and case fields:

  • invoiceNumber → updated
  • customerNumber → auto‑identified
  • isClearing → set according to logic (e.g., invoice found, ready to post)

This gives the ESM user immediate visibility:

  • Has the system recognized the invoice?
  • Has the customer been identified?
  • Is the invoice ready for posting?

Step 6: ESM Sends the Validated Invoice to S/4HANA via OData APIs

Once the case has all mandatory and validated data, the ESM user can trigger the next action:

“Post Invoice to S/4”

At this point:

  • ESM calls S/4HANA OData APIs
  • A draft or final FI invoice is created
  • Additional steps like clearing, matching, or workflow approvals occur inside S/4

This hands-off process ensures:

  • No manual entry in S/4
  • No data inconsistencies
  • Full auditability from source → document → posting

Step 7: ESM User Monitors and Completes the Process

Inside the ESM case, the user can now see:

  • Status of the posting
  • Document number returned from S/4
  • Any errors (e.g., master data inconsistencies)
  • New actions available for follow-up

They can then:

  • Update the customer
  • Add missing reference information
  • Re-trigger the posting
  • Close the case once processing is complete

Result: A Fully Streamlined, AI‑Driven Invoice Workflow

By integrating SAP ESM, Document AI, and S/4HANA, organizations benefit from:

✔ Zero manual invoice entry

✔ Auto-identification of customers and invoice data

✔ Early error detection using business rules

✔ Faster posting to S/4HANA

✔ End-to-end transparency in one central case

✔ Higher accuracy and reduced effort

This modernizes invoice handling from a traditionally manual process into an intelligent, automated workflow.

Learning Journey :  Business Documents in ESM

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Service Management [ESM] - How to enable Business Document
entry.content:
Previous Blog : How SAP ESM + Document AI + S/4HANA Work Together to Automate Invoice Processing End‑to‑End

Business Documents

Business Document feature available in SAP Enterprise Service Management SKU (ESM)

Business Documents represent back-end records in a semantic way and can create, update, or display those records. Values in the table can be populated automatically using AI services or entered manually.

Business documents can capture data from email message or case subject and description, attachments of various file types, or using manual data entry. Attachment data and entity information is extracted with generative AI using large language models.

You, as an administrator, can create business document schemas to represent data patterns for different scenarios. Schemas specify which data to use in your business documents and how you want the data formatted in your solution. If you've enabled business documents for a specific case type, you can find the Business Documents tab on the case detail view. Use conditions to make specific business document fields required, read only, or hidden.

Business Document Integration

Create an outbound configuration to enable actions to send or retrieve data from external systems, or to populate values in dropdown menus in business documents based on a dynamic data source.

Prerequisites

Set up an outbound communication system under System Settings -  Integration  - Communication Systems.

Procedure

Go to your user menu and choose System Settings  - Business Documents - Business Document Integration.

  • Choose + (Create).
  • Enter a name and select the communication system.
  • Choose Search and Add to add existing HTTP data outbound configurations or + (Create) to create a new configuration.

Create Business Documents

Select + Create Business Document, Enter the required information, and save

Your new business document appears in the Business Documents list with the header details on the right. A new draft version of the business document appears in the Versions table. Select + Actions to activate the version, copy to a new business document, create new version, or delete the version. There can only be one draft version. You can activate multiple versions but there must only be one active version within the specified validity period.

Select + in the Designer tab to add controls and fields to the business document table.

Add actions to the business document. Go to the Actions tab and select + Add Action.

Go to the Input Options tab to choose one or more input methods for your business document.

Configure Business Document Extraction Schemas

Use schemas to configure document extraction from attachments.

Add Business Document to Case Type

  1. Go to User Menu > System Settings  > All Settings  > Cases  > Case Types.
  2. Select a case type.
  3. Select  (Open in Detail View) to open the case type details.
  4. Switch on Business Documents in the Features Settings section

  1. Choose the Additional Features tab in the case type header.
  2. In the Business Documents section, choose the  (Search) icon to search for a business document to add to this case type.
  3. Choose + (Add) to add the business document to the case type.

    You can assign only one business document to each case type. When a case of this type is created, the Business Documents tab appears in the case detail view.

entry.title: What's New in SAP S/4HANA 2025 Private Cloud - Detail Analysis
